infobox.value: ([/ˈɡrɛnɪtʃ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-uk-LBGreenwich.ogg "File:En-uk-LBGreenwich.ogg") [*GREN-itch*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), [/-ɪdʒ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*-⁠ij*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), [/ˈɡrɪn-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*GRIN-*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)) is an [affluent](/wiki/Affluent "Affluent")[[3]](#cite_note-Telegraph_Best_Places_London-3) [area](/wiki/List_of_areas_of_London "List of areas of London") in south-east [London](/wiki/London "London"), England, within the [Royal Borough of Greenwich](/wiki/Royal_Borough_of_Greenwich "Royal Borough of Greenwich") and the [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England") of [Greater London](/wiki/Greater_London "Greater London"), 5.5 miles (8.9 km) east-south-east of [Charing Cross](/wiki/Charing_Cross "Charing Cross"). It contains the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") of [Maritime Greenwich](#World_Heritage_Site).

**Greenwich** ([/ˈɡrɛnɪtʃ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-uk-LBGreenwich.ogg "File:En-uk-LBGreenwich.ogg") [*GREN-itch*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), [/-ɪdʒ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*-⁠ij*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), [/ˈɡrɪn-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*GRIN-*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)) is an [affluent](/wiki/Affluent "Affluent")[[3]](#cite_note-Telegraph_Best_Places_London-3) [area](/wiki/List_of_areas_of_London "List of areas of London") in south-east [London](/wiki/London "London"), England, within the [Royal Borough of Greenwich](/wiki/Royal_Borough_of_Greenwich "Royal Borough of Greenwich") and the [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England") of [Greater London](/wiki/Greater_London "Greater London"), 5.5 miles (8.9 km) east-south-east of [Charing Cross](/wiki/Charing_Cross "Charing Cross"). It contains the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") of [Maritime Greenwich](#World_Heritage_Site).

Greenwich is notable for its [astronomical](/wiki/History_of_astronomy "History of astronomy") and [maritime history](/wiki/Maritime_history "Maritime history") and for giving its name to the [Greenwich Meridian](/wiki/Prime_meridian_(Greenwich) "Prime meridian (Greenwich)") (0° longitude) and [Greenwich Mean Time](/wiki/Greenwich_Mean_Time "Greenwich Mean Time"). The town became the site of a royal palace, the [Palace of Placentia](/wiki/Palace_of_Placentia "Palace of Placentia"), from the 15th century and was the birthplace of many [Tudors](/wiki/House_of_Tudor "House of Tudor"), including [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII_of_England "Henry VIII of England") and [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I"). The palace fell into disrepair during the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War") and was demolished, eventually being replaced by the [Royal Naval Hospital for Sailors](/wiki/Greenwich_Hospital_(London) "Greenwich Hospital (London)"), designed by Sir [Christopher Wren](/wiki/Christopher_Wren "Christopher Wren") and his assistant [Nicholas Hawksmoor](/wiki/Nicholas_Hawksmoor "Nicholas Hawksmoor"). These buildings became the [Royal Naval College](/wiki/Old_Royal_Naval_College "Old Royal Naval College") in 1873, and they remained a military education establishment until 1998, when they passed into the hands of the Greenwich Foundation. The historic rooms within these buildings remain open to the public; other buildings are used by the [University of Greenwich](/wiki/University_of_Greenwich "University of Greenwich") and [Trinity Laban Conservatoire of Music and Dance](/wiki/Trinity_Laban_Conservatoire_of_Music_and_Dance "Trinity Laban Conservatoire of Music and Dance").

The town became a popular resort in the 18th century, and many grand houses were built there, such as [Vanbrugh Castle](/wiki/Vanbrugh_Castle "Vanbrugh Castle") (1717) on [Maze Hill](/wiki/Maze_Hill "Maze Hill") and the [Ranger's House](/wiki/Ranger%27s_House "Ranger's House") (1722) near [Blackheath](/wiki/Blackheath,_London "Blackheath, London"). From the [Georgian period](/wiki/Georgian_period "Georgian period") estates of houses were constructed above the town centre. The maritime connections of Greenwich were celebrated in the 20th century, with the siting of the historic vessels *[Cutty Sark](/wiki/Cutty_Sark "Cutty Sark")* and *[Gipsy Moth IV](/wiki/Gipsy_Moth_IV "Gipsy Moth IV")* next to the river front, and the [National Maritime Museum](/wiki/National_Maritime_Museum "National Maritime Museum") in the former buildings of the [Royal Hospital School](/wiki/Royal_Hospital_School "Royal Hospital School") in 1934.

Historically an [ancient parish](/wiki/Ancient_parish "Ancient parish") in the [Blackheath Hundred](/wiki/Hundred_of_Blackheath,_Kent "Hundred of Blackheath, Kent") of [Kent](/wiki/Kent "Kent"), the town formed part of the growing [conurbation](/wiki/Conurbation "Conurbation") of [Victorian London](/wiki/Victorian_London "Victorian London") in the 19th century. When the [County of London](/wiki/County_of_London "County of London"), an [administrative area](/wiki/Administrative_counties_of_England "Administrative counties of England") designed to replace the [Metropolitan Board of Works](/wiki/Metropolitan_Board_of_Works "Metropolitan Board of Works"), was formed in 1889, the parish merged with those of [Charlton-next-Woolwich](/wiki/Charlton,_London "Charlton, London"), [Deptford St Nicholas](/wiki/Deptford_St_Nicholas "Deptford St Nicholas") and [Kidbrooke](/wiki/Kidbrooke "Kidbrooke") to create the [Metropolitan Borough of Greenwich](/wiki/Metropolitan_Borough_of_Greenwich "Metropolitan Borough of Greenwich"). When local government in London was again [reformed in 1965](/wiki/London_Government_Act_1963 "London Government Act 1963"), it merged with most of the [Metropolitan Borough of Woolwich](/wiki/Metropolitan_Borough_of_Woolwich "Metropolitan Borough of Woolwich"), creating what is now the [Royal Borough of Greenwich](/wiki/Royal_Borough_of_Greenwich "Royal Borough of Greenwich"), a [local authority district](/wiki/Districts_of_England "Districts of England") of Greater London.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Toponymy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Toponymy")]

The place-name 'Greenwich' is first attested in an [Anglo-Saxon charter](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_charter "Anglo-Saxon charter") of 918, where it appears as *Gronewic*. It is recorded as *Grenewic* in 964, and as *Grenawic* in the [Anglo-Saxon Chronicle](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_Chronicle "Anglo-Saxon Chronicle") for 1013. It is *Grenviz* in the [Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book") of 1086, and *Grenewych* in the *[Taxatio Ecclesiastica](/wiki/Taxatio_Ecclesiastica "Taxatio Ecclesiastica")* of 1291. The name means 'green *wic'*, indicating that Greenwich was what is known as a [-wich town](/wiki/-wich_town "-wich town") or [emporium](/wiki/Emporium_(early_medieval) "Emporium (early medieval)"), from the Latin '[vicus](/wiki/Vicus "Vicus")'.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The settlement later became known as *East Greenwich* to distinguish it from *West Greenwich* or *Deptford Strond*, the part of [Deptford](/wiki/Deptford "Deptford") adjacent to the [River Thames](/wiki/River_Thames "River Thames"),[[5]](#cite_note-Parish-5) but the use of *East Greenwich* to mean the whole of the town of Greenwich died out in the 19th century. However, Greenwich was divided into the registration subdistricts of *Greenwich East* and *Greenwich West* from the beginning of [civil registration](/wiki/Civil_registration "Civil registration") in 1837, the boundary running down what is now [Greenwich Church Street](/wiki/Greenwich_Church_Street "Greenwich Church Street") and [Croom's Hill](/wiki/Croom%27s_Hill "Croom's Hill"), although more modern references to "*East*" and "*West*" Greenwich probably refer to the areas east and west of the [Royal Naval College](/wiki/Old_Royal_Naval_College "Old Royal Naval College") and [National Maritime Museum](/wiki/National_Maritime_Museum "National Maritime Museum") corresponding with the West Greenwich [council ward](/wiki/Ward_(politics) "Ward (politics)"). An article in *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")* of 13 October 1967 stated:[[6]](#cite_note-6)

> *East Greenwich*, gateway to the [Blackwall Tunnel](/wiki/Blackwall_Tunnel "Blackwall Tunnel"), remains solidly [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class"), the manpower for one eighth of London's [heavy industry](/wiki/Heavy_industry "Heavy industry"). *West Greenwich* is a hybrid: the spirit of Nelson, the *Cutty Sark*, the Maritime Museum, an industrial waterfront and a number of elegant houses, ripe for development.

#### Manor of East Greenwich

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Manor of East Greenwich")]

[Royal charters](/wiki/Colonial_charters_in_the_Thirteen_Colonies "Colonial charters in the Thirteen Colonies") granted to English colonists in North America,[[7]](#cite_note-7) as well as in [Company Bombay](/wiki/History_of_Bombay_under_British_rule_(1661%E2%80%931947) "History of Bombay under British rule (1661–1947)") and [St Helena](/wiki/St_Helena "St Helena"),[[8]](#cite_note-8) often used the name of the [manor](/wiki/Manorialism "Manorialism") of East Greenwich for describing the tenure (from the Latin verb *teneo*, hold) as that of free [socage](/wiki/Socage "Socage").[[9]](#cite_note-9) [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") charters provided that the grantees should hold their lands "as of his Majesty's manor of East Greenwich". This was in relation to the principle of land tenure under English law, that the ruling monarch (king or queen) was paramount lord of all the soil in the *terra regis*,[[10]](#cite_note-10) while all others held their lands, directly or indirectly, under the monarch. Land outside the physical boundaries of England, as in America, was treated as belonging constructively to one of the existing royal manors, and from Tudor times grants frequently used the name of the manor of East Greenwich,[[11]](#cite_note-11) but some 17th-century grants named [the castle of Windsor](/wiki/Windsor_Castle "Windsor Castle").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) Places in North America that have taken the name "East Greenwich" include [a township in Gloucester County, New Jersey](/wiki/East_Greenwich_Township,_New_Jersey "East Greenwich Township, New Jersey"), [a hamlet in Washington County, New York](/wiki/East_Greenwich,_New_York "East Greenwich, New York"), and [a town in Kent County, Rhode Island](/wiki/East_Greenwich,_Rhode_Island "East Greenwich, Rhode Island"). [Greenwich, Connecticut](/wiki/Greenwich,_Connecticut "Greenwich, Connecticut") was also named after Greenwich.

### Early settlement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early settlement")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Burial_mounds_in_Greenwich_Park.jpg/500px-Burial_mounds_in_Greenwich_Park.jpg)](/wiki/File:Burial_mounds_in_Greenwich_Park.jpg)

Prehistoric burial mounds in [Greenwich Park](/wiki/Greenwich_Park "Greenwich Park")

[Tumuli](/wiki/Tumulus "Tumulus") to the south-west of Flamsteed House,[[15]](#cite_note-15) in [Greenwich Park](/wiki/Greenwich_Park "Greenwich Park"), are thought to be early [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") barrows re-used by the Saxons in the 6th century as burial grounds. To the east between the Vanbrugh and Maze Hill Gates is the site of a Roman villa or temple. A small area of red paving [tesserae](/wiki/Tesserae "Tesserae") protected by railings marks the spot. It was excavated in 1902, and 300 coins were found dating from the emperors [Claudius](/wiki/Claudius "Claudius") and [Honorius](/wiki/Honorius_(emperor) "Honorius (emperor)") to the 5th century. This was excavated by the [Channel 4](/wiki/Channel_4 "Channel 4") television programme *[Time Team](/wiki/Time_Team "Time Team")* in 1999, broadcast in 2000,[[16]](#cite_note-16) and further investigations were made by the same group in 2003.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The [Roman road](/wiki/Roman_road "Roman road") from London to [Dover](/wiki/Dover "Dover"), [Watling Street](/wiki/Watling_Street "Watling Street"), crossed the high ground to the south of Greenwich, through Blackheath. This followed the line of an earlier [Celtic](/wiki/Celt "Celt") route from [Canterbury](/wiki/Canterbury "Canterbury") to [St Albans](/wiki/St_Albans "St Albans").[[18]](#cite_note-18) As late as [Henry V](/wiki/Henry_V_of_England "Henry V of England"), Greenwich was only a fishing town, with a safe anchorage in the river.[[5]](#cite_note-Parish-5)

### Viking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Viking")]

During the reign of [Æthelred the Unready](/wiki/%C3%86thelred_the_Unready "Æthelred the Unready"), the [Danish](/wiki/Danes_(Germanic_tribe) "Danes (Germanic tribe)") fleet anchored in the River Thames off Greenwich for over three years, and the army encamped on the hill above. From here they attacked [Kent](/wiki/Kent "Kent") and, in the year 1012, took the city of Canterbury, making Archbishop [Alphege](/wiki/Alphege "Alphege") their prisoner for seven months in their camp at Greenwich, at that time within the county of Kent. They stoned him to death for his refusal to allow his ransom (3,000 pieces of silver) to be paid; and kept his body, until the reported blossoming of a stick that had been immersed in his blood. For this miracle his body was released to his followers, he achieved sainthood for his martyrdom and, in the 12th century, the parish church was dedicated to him. The present church on the site west of the town centre is [St Alfege's Church](/wiki/St_Alfege_Church,_Greenwich "St Alfege Church, Greenwich"), designed by [Nicholas Hawksmoor](/wiki/Nicholas_Hawksmoor "Nicholas Hawksmoor") in 1714 and completed in 1718.

### Norman

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Norman")]

The [Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book") of 1086 records the manor of *Grenviz* in the [hundred](/wiki/Hundred_(county_division) "Hundred (county division)") of *Grenviz* as held by Bishop [Odo of Bayeux](/wiki/Odo_of_Bayeux "Odo of Bayeux");[[19]](#cite_note-19) his lands were seized by the crown in 1082. The name of the hundred was changed to [Blackheath](/wiki/Hundred_of_Blackheath,_Kent "Hundred of Blackheath, Kent") when the site of the hundred court was moved there in the 12th century. There has been a royal palace or hunting lodge here since before 1300, when [Edward I](/wiki/Edward_I "Edward I") is known to have made offerings at the chapel of the Virgin Mary.[[20]](#cite_note-Green-20)

### Plantagenet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Plantagenet")]

Subsequent monarchs were regular visitors, with [Henry IV](/wiki/Henry_IV_of_England "Henry IV of England") making his will here, and [Henry V](/wiki/Henry_V,_King_of_England "Henry V, King of England") granting the manor, for life, to [Thomas Beaufort, Duke of Exeter](/wiki/Thomas_Beaufort,_Duke_of_Exeter "Thomas Beaufort, Duke of Exeter"), who died at Greenwich in 1426. The palace was created by [Humphrey, Duke of Gloucester](/wiki/Humphrey,_Duke_of_Gloucester "Humphrey, Duke of Gloucester"), Henry V's younger brother and regent to his son [Henry VI](/wiki/Henry_VI_of_England "Henry VI of England") in 1447; he enclosed the park and erected a tower ([Greenwich Castle](/wiki/Greenwich_Castle "Greenwich Castle")) on the hill now occupied by the [Royal Observatory](/wiki/Royal_Observatory,_Greenwich "Royal Observatory, Greenwich"). The Thames-side palace was renamed the [Palace of Placentia](/wiki/Palace_of_Placentia "Palace of Placentia") or Pleasaunce by Henry VI's consort [Margaret of Anjou](/wiki/Margaret_of_Anjou "Margaret of Anjou") after Humphrey's death. The palace was completed and further enlarged by [Edward IV](/wiki/Edward_IV_of_England "Edward IV of England"), and in 1466 it was granted to his queen, [Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth_Woodville "Elizabeth Woodville").[[20]](#cite_note-Green-20) Edward IV had previously been given permission by the Pope to establish a [Franciscan](/wiki/Franciscans "Franciscans") [friary](/wiki/Friary "Friary") of [Observant Friars](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor "Order of Friars Minor") in Greenwich, this was done in 1485, two years after his death; the first Observant House in England, it was located on land adjacent to the palace.[[21]](#cite_note-HERR-21) After rejecting papal authority in 1534, the Franciscan Observants were suppressed; refounded as Franciscan Conventual, the friary was dissolved in 1538, then re-established in 1555 for Observants, before the friars were finally expelled in 1559 and the friary was demolished in 1662.[[21]](#cite_note-HERR-21)

Ultimately it was because the palace and its grounds were a royal possession (with a useful hill) that it was chosen as the site for [Charles II's](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") Royal Observatory, from which stemmed Greenwich's subsequent global role as originator of the modern [Prime Meridian](/wiki/Prime_Meridian "Prime Meridian").

### Tudor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Tudor")]

The palace was the principal residence of [Henry VII](/wiki/Henry_VII_of_England "Henry VII of England") whose sons Henry (later [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII_of_England "Henry VIII of England")) and [Edmund Tudor](/wiki/Edmund_Tudor,_Duke_of_Somerset "Edmund Tudor, Duke of Somerset") were born here, and baptised in St Alphege's. Henry favoured Greenwich over nearby [Eltham Palace](/wiki/Eltham_Palace "Eltham Palace"), the former principal royal palace in south London, which was not on the River Thames and so was less accessible. Henry extended Greenwich Palace and it became his principal London seat until [Whitehall Palace](/wiki/Whitehall_Palace "Whitehall Palace") was built in the 1530s. Henry VIII married [Catherine of Aragon](/wiki/Catherine_of_Aragon "Catherine of Aragon") and [Anne of Cleves](/wiki/Anne_of_Cleves "Anne of Cleves") at Greenwich, and both of his daughters, [Mary](/wiki/Mary_I_of_England "Mary I of England") (18 February 1516) and [Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth_I_of_England "Elizabeth I of England") (7 September 1533), were born at Greenwich. His son [Edward VI](/wiki/Edward_VI "Edward VI") also died there at age 15.

The palace of Placentia, in turn, became Elizabeth's favourite summer residence.[[20]](#cite_note-Green-20) Both she and her sister Mary used the palace extensively, and Elizabeth's Council planned the [Spanish Armada](/wiki/Spanish_Armada "Spanish Armada") campaign there in 1588.

### Stuart

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Stuart")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Adriaen_van_Stalbemt_-_A_View_of_Greenwich.jpg/500px-Adriaen_van_Stalbemt_-_A_View_of_Greenwich.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adriaen_van_Stalbemt_-_A_View_of_Greenwich.jpg)

[Adriaen van Stalbemt](/wiki/Adriaen_van_Stalbemt "Adriaen van Stalbemt")'s *A View of Greenwich*, c. 1632, showing [King Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") (in the black hat) and his family. [Greenwich Palace](/wiki/Greenwich_Palace "Greenwich Palace") can be seen in front of the [River Thames](/wiki/River_Thames "River Thames") behind them. [Royal Collection](/wiki/Royal_Collection "Royal Collection"), London.

[James I](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I") carried out the final remodelling work on Greenwich Palace, granting the manor to his wife [Queen Anne of Denmark](/wiki/Anne_of_Denmark "Anne of Denmark"). In 1616 Anne commissioned [Inigo Jones](/wiki/Inigo_Jones "Inigo Jones") to design and build the surviving [Queen's House](/wiki/Queen%27s_House "Queen's House") as the final addition to the palace.

[Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") granted the manor to his wife [Queen Henrietta Maria](/wiki/Queen_Henrietta_Maria "Queen Henrietta Maria"), for whom Inigo Jones completed the Queen's House. During the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"), the palace was used as a biscuit factory and prisoner-of-war camp. Then, in the [Interregnum](/wiki/English_Interregnum "English Interregnum"), the palace and park were seized to become a 'mansion' for the [Lord Protector](/wiki/Lord_Protector "Lord Protector").

By the time of the [Restoration](/wiki/Restoration_(1660) "Restoration (1660)"), the Palace of Placentia had fallen into disuse and was pulled down. New buildings began to be established as a grand palace for [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England"), but only the King Charles block was completed. Charles II also redesigned and replanted Greenwich Park and founded and built the Royal Observatory.

Prince James (later King [James II & VII](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England")), as [Duke of York](/wiki/Duke_of_York "Duke of York") and [Lord High Admiral](/wiki/Lord_High_Admiral_of_the_United_Kingdom "Lord High Admiral of the United Kingdom") until 1673, was often at Greenwich with his brother Charles and, according to [Samuel Pepys](/wiki/Samuel_Pepys "Samuel Pepys"), he proposed the idea of creating a Royal Naval Hospital. This was eventually established at Greenwich by his daughter [Mary II](/wiki/Mary_II_of_England "Mary II of England"), who in 1692–1693 commissioned [Christopher Wren](/wiki/Christopher_Wren "Christopher Wren") to design the Royal Hospital for Seamen (now the Old Royal Naval College). The work was begun under her widower [William III](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") in 1696 and completed by [Hawksmoor](/wiki/Hawksmoor "Hawksmoor"). [Queen Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain") and [Prince George of Denmark](/wiki/Prince_George_of_Denmark "Prince George of Denmark") continued to patronise the project.

### Hanoverian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Hanoverian")]

[George I](/wiki/George_I_of_Great_Britain "George I of Great Britain") landed at Greenwich from Hanover on his accession in 1714. His successor [George II](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain") granted the Royal Hospital for Seamen the forfeited estates of the [Jacobite](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1715 "Jacobite rising of 1715") [Earl of Derwentwater](/wiki/James_Radclyffe,_3rd_Earl_of_Derwentwater "James Radclyffe, 3rd Earl of Derwentwater"), which allowed the building to be completed by 1751.

In 1805, [George III](/wiki/George_III_of_the_United_Kingdom "George III of the United Kingdom") granted the Queen's House to the [Royal Naval Asylum](/wiki/Royal_Naval_Asylum "Royal Naval Asylum") (an orphanage school), which amalgamated in 1821–1825 with the Greenwich Hospital School. Extended with the buildings that now house the National Maritime Museum, it was renamed the [Royal Hospital School](/wiki/Royal_Hospital_School "Royal Hospital School") by [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") in 1892.

[George IV](/wiki/George_IV "George IV") donated nearly 40 paintings to the hospital in 1824, at a stroke creating a gallery in the [Painted Hall](/wiki/Painted_Hall "Painted Hall"). These now form the Greenwich Hospital Collection at the National Maritime Museum. Subsequently, [William IV](/wiki/William_IV_of_the_United_Kingdom "William IV of the United Kingdom") and [Queen Adelaide](/wiki/Adelaide_of_Saxe-Meiningen "Adelaide of Saxe-Meiningen") were both regular donors and visitors to the gallery.

### Victorian and Edwardian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Victorian and Edwardian")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Descriptive_map_of_London_poverty%2C_1889_Wellcome_L0027751.jpg/250px-Descriptive_map_of_London_poverty%2C_1889_Wellcome_L0027751.jpg)](/wiki/File:Descriptive_map_of_London_poverty,_1889_Wellcome_L0027751.jpg)

In the 1880s, if this place is so cut into three: east, central and west zones of about 30,000 inhabitants each, the central one had less than 10% recognisable poverty, the minimum of London's map above, but the others (east and west) more than 40%.

Queen Victoria rarely visited Greenwich, but in 1845 her husband [Prince Albert](/wiki/Albert,_Prince_Consort "Albert, Prince Consort") personally bought [Nelson's](/wiki/Admiral_Nelson "Admiral Nelson") [Trafalgar](/wiki/Battle_of_Trafalgar "Battle of Trafalgar") coat for the Naval Gallery.

In 1838 the [London and Greenwich Railway](/wiki/London_and_Greenwich_Railway "London and Greenwich Railway") (L&GR) completed the first steam railway in London. It started at London Bridge and had its terminus at London Street (now Greenwich High Road). It was also the first to be built specifically for passengers, and the first elevated railway, having 878 arches over its almost four mile stretch. South of the railway's viaduct over [Deptford Creek](/wiki/Deptford_Creek "Deptford Creek") is [a Victorian pumping station](/wiki/Deptford_pumping_station "Deptford pumping station") constructed in 1864 as part of [Joseph Bazalgette](/wiki/Joseph_Bazalgette "Joseph Bazalgette")'s [London sewerage system](/wiki/London_sewerage_system "London sewerage system") (the Southern Outfall Sewer flows under Greenwich town centre).

In 1853 the local Scottish Presbyterian community built a church, St Mark's, nearby which was extended twice in the 1860s during the ministry of [Adolph Saphir](/wiki/Adolph_Saphir "Adolph Saphir"), eventually accommodating 1,000 worshippers.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In 1864 opposite the railway terminus, theatrical entrepreneur [Sefton Parry](/wiki/Sefton_Henry_Parry "Sefton Henry Parry") built the thousand-seater [New Greenwich Theatre](/wiki/Greenwich_Theatre "Greenwich Theatre").[[23]](#cite_note-23) [William Morton](/wiki/William_Morton_(theatre_manager) "William Morton (theatre manager)") was one of its more successful managers. The theatre was demolished in 1937 to make way for a [new Town Hall](/wiki/Greenwich_Town_Hall,_London "Greenwich Town Hall, London"), now a listed building under new ownership and renamed Meridian House.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Our_Ladye_Star_of_the_Sea%2C_Greenwich.jpg/250px-Our_Ladye_Star_of_the_Sea%2C_Greenwich.jpg)](/wiki/File:Our_Ladye_Star_of_the_Sea,_Greenwich.jpg)

Our Ladye Star of the Sea

Greenwich Station is at the northern apex of the Ashburnham Triangle, a residential estate developed by the Ashburnham family, mainly between 1830 and 1870, on land previously developed as market gardens. It is now a designated conservation area.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The present Greenwich Theatre, further to the east, on Croom's Hill, was constructed inside the shell of a Victorian music hall. Beginning life in 1855 as an annexe to the Rose and Crown, the music hall was rebuilt in 1871 by Charles Crowder and subsequently operated under many names. Further south on Croom's Hill, the Roman Catholic church of [Our Ladye Star of the Sea](/wiki/Our_Ladye_Star_of_the_Sea "Our Ladye Star of the Sea") was opened in 1851.[[26]](#cite_note-TStock-26)

The meridian was established in 1851.

### Modern and the present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Modern and the present")]

[George V](/wiki/George_V "George V") and his wife [Queen Mary](/wiki/Mary_of_Teck "Mary of Teck") both supported the creation of the National Maritime Museum, and Mary presented the museum with many items.

Prince Albert, Duke of York (later [George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI")), laid the foundation stone of the new Royal Hospital School when it moved out to [Holbrook](/wiki/Holbrook,_Suffolk "Holbrook, Suffolk"), Suffolk. In 1937 his first public act as king, three weeks before his coronation, was to open the National Maritime Museum in the buildings vacated by the school. The king was accompanied by his mother Queen Mary, his wife [Queen Elizabeth](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_The_Queen_Mother "Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother") and [Princess Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II").

Princess Elizabeth and her consort [Prince Philip](/wiki/Prince_Philip,_Duke_of_Edinburgh "Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh"), who had been ennobled [Duke of Edinburgh](/wiki/Duke_of_Edinburgh "Duke of Edinburgh") and [Baron Greenwich](/wiki/Baron_Greenwich "Baron Greenwich") on marriage in 1947, made their first public and official visit to Greenwich in 1948 to receive the Freedom of the Borough for Philip. In the same year, he became a trustee of the National Maritime Museum. Prince Philip was a trustee for 52 years until 2000, when he became its first [patron](/wiki/Patron "Patron"). The Duke of Edinburgh was also a patron of the *[Cutty Sark](/wiki/Cutty_Sark "Cutty Sark")* (which was opened by the Queen in 1957) from 1952.

During the [Silver Jubilee of 1977](/wiki/Silver_Jubilee_of_Elizabeth_II "Silver Jubilee of Elizabeth II"), the Queen embarked at Greenwich for the Jubilee River Pageant. In 1987, she was aboard the [P&O](/wiki/P%26O "P&O") ship *[Pacific Princess](/wiki/MS_Pacific "MS Pacific")* when it moored alongside the Old Royal Naval College for the college's 150th-anniversary celebrations.

To mark the [Diamond Jubilee of Elizabeth II](/wiki/Diamond_Jubilee_of_Elizabeth_II "Diamond Jubilee of Elizabeth II"), on 3 February 2012 the [Borough of Greenwich](/wiki/Royal_Borough_of_Greenwich "Royal Borough of Greenwich") became the fourth district and third London Borough to have Royal Borough status, the others being [Kingston upon Thames](/wiki/Royal_Borough_of_Kingston_upon_Thames "Royal Borough of Kingston upon Thames"), [Kensington & Chelsea](/wiki/Royal_Borough_of_Kensington_%26_Chelsea "Royal Borough of Kensington & Chelsea") and [Windsor & Maidenhead](/wiki/Royal_Borough_of_Windsor_%26_Maidenhead "Royal Borough of Windsor & Maidenhead").[[27]](#cite_note-27) The status was granted in recognition of the borough's historic links with the [monarchy](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom"), the location of the Prime Meridian and its being a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site").[[28]](#cite_note-royal-28)

## Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Governance")]

Main articles: [Royal Borough of Greenwich](/wiki/Royal_Borough_of_Greenwich "Royal Borough of Greenwich"), [Greenwich Park (ward)](/wiki/Greenwich_Park_(ward) "Greenwich Park (ward)"), and [Greenwich London Borough Council](/wiki/Greenwich_London_Borough_Council "Greenwich London Borough Council")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Meridian_House_%28Former_Greenwich_Town_Hall%29.jpg/250px-Meridian_House_%28Former_Greenwich_Town_Hall%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Meridian_House_(Former_Greenwich_Town_Hall).jpg)

The former [Greenwich Town Hall](/wiki/Greenwich_Town_Hall,_London "Greenwich Town Hall, London"), now known as "Meridian House"

Greenwich is covered by the Greenwich West and Peninsula wards of the [London Borough of Greenwich](/wiki/Royal_Borough_of_Greenwich "Royal Borough of Greenwich"), which was formed in 1965 by merging the former [Metropolitan Borough of Greenwich](/wiki/Metropolitan_Borough_of_Greenwich "Metropolitan Borough of Greenwich") with that part of the [Metropolitan Borough of Woolwich](/wiki/Metropolitan_Borough_of_Woolwich "Metropolitan Borough of Woolwich") which lay south of the River Thames. Along with [Blackheath](/wiki/Blackheath,_London "Blackheath, London") Westcombe, [Charlton](/wiki/Charlton,_London "Charlton, London"), Glyndon, [Woolwich](/wiki/Woolwich "Woolwich") Riverside, and [Woolwich Common](/wiki/Woolwich_Common "Woolwich Common"), it elects a [Member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (MP) for [Greenwich and Woolwich](/wiki/Greenwich_and_Woolwich "Greenwich and Woolwich"); [Matthew Pennycook](/wiki/Matthew_Pennycook "Matthew Pennycook") was elected as its MP in 2015 and later elections.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

The London Borough of Greenwich became the [Royal Borough of Greenwich](/wiki/Royal_Borough_of_Greenwich "Royal Borough of Greenwich") to mark the [Diamond Jubilee of Elizabeth II](/wiki/Diamond_Jubilee_of_Elizabeth_II "Diamond Jubilee of Elizabeth II") on 3 February 2012 due to the links of the borough with the [royal family](/wiki/British_royal_family "British royal family"). Greenwich is represented in the [Greenwich London Borough Council](/wiki/Greenwich_London_Borough_Council "Greenwich London Borough Council") by the councillors of the [Greenwich Park Ward](/wiki/Greenwich_Park_(ward) "Greenwich Park (ward)"), [East Greenwich Ward](/wiki/East_Greenwich_(ward) "East Greenwich (ward)"), and the [Greenwich Creekside Ward](/wiki/Greenwich_Creekside_(ward) "Greenwich Creekside (ward)").[[30]](#cite_note-30)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Demographics")]

### Ethnic groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Ethnic groups")]

Ethnic composition ([2011](/wiki/2011_United_Kingdom_census "2011 United Kingdom census"))[[31]](#cite_note-West_Greenwich_Ward-31)

| Ethnic group | Population | % |
| --- | --- | --- |
| White | 11,029 | 66.7 |
| Black or Black British | 2,393 | 14.5 |
| Asian or Asian British | 1,808 | 10.9 |
| Mixed or Multiple ethnic groups | 980 | 5.9 |
| Other ethnic group | 317 | 1.9 |
| **Total** | **16,527** | **100** |

In comparison with [Greater London](/wiki/Greater_London "Greater London") as a whole, West Greenwich has a relatively higher proportion of residents identifying as White, particularly [White British](/wiki/White_British "White British"), while still remaining ethnically diverse by national standards.[[31]](#cite_note-West_Greenwich_Ward-31)[[32]](#cite_note-London_Demographics-32) The ward's Black or [Black British](/wiki/Black_British "Black British") population, especially those identifying as Black African, is broadly in line with or slightly above the London average, reflecting established African diaspora communities in south-east London.[[31]](#cite_note-West_Greenwich_Ward-31)[[32]](#cite_note-London_Demographics-32) Asian or [Asian British](/wiki/Asian_British "Asian British") residents form a smaller share of the population than across London overall, although the proportion identifying as Chinese is comparatively notable. Mixed or Multiple ethnic groups are represented at similar levels to the London average, while the proportion of residents identifying with Other ethnic groups is slightly lower. Overall, the ethnic composition of West Greenwich reflects a more residential inner-London profile, combining a strong White British presence with significant minority communities.

### Employment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Employment")]

The employment profile of West Greenwich residents is characterised by a strong concentration in service-based industries, particularly [financial and insurance activities](/wiki/Financial_services "Financial services") due to the transport links to [Canary Wharf](/wiki/Canary_Wharf "Canary Wharf") and the [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London").[[31]](#cite_note-West_Greenwich_Ward-31) Professional, scientific and technical services account for a higher proportion of employment than the London average.[[31]](#cite_note-West_Greenwich_Ward-31)[[32]](#cite_note-London_Demographics-32) [Education](/wiki/Education "Education") and [human health](/wiki/Healthcare_industry "Healthcare industry") and social work activities also represent significant shares of local employment, reflecting the presence of public and institutional employers. In contrast, sectors such as construction, wholesale and retail trade, and transport and storage employ a smaller proportion of residents in West Greenwich compared with Greater London overall. Employment in primary and industrial sectors, including agriculture, mining, manufacturing and utilities, remains minimal, consistent with the ward's urban character.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Geography")]

### Geography of Greenwich

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Geography of Greenwich")]

The town of Greenwich is built on a broad platform to the south of the outside of a broad meander in the River Thames, with a safe deep water anchorage lying in the river. To the south, the land rises steeply, 100 feet (30 m) through Greenwich Park to the town of Blackheath. The higher areas consist of a sedimentary layer of gravelly soils, known as the *Blackheath Beds*, that spread through much of the south-east over a chalk outcrop—with sands, loam and seams of clay at the lower levels by the river.

Greenwich is bordered by [Deptford Creek](/wiki/River_Ravensbourne "River Ravensbourne") and Deptford to the west; the residential area of [Westcombe Park](/wiki/Westcombe_Park "Westcombe Park") to the east; the River Thames to the north; and the [A2](/wiki/A2_road_(Great_Britain) "A2 road (Great Britain)") and Blackheath to the south. The [Greenwich Peninsula](/wiki/Greenwich_Peninsula "Greenwich Peninsula"), northeast of the town centre and also known as North Greenwich, forms the main projection of the town.

### Nearby areas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Nearby areas")]

Across the [River Thames](/wiki/River_Thames "River Thames") to the north lies the [Isle of Dogs](/wiki/Isle_of_Dogs "Isle of Dogs"), a riverside district that includes [Millwall](/wiki/Millwall "Millwall"), a largely residential area, and [Canary Wharf](/wiki/Canary_Wharf "Canary Wharf"), one of London's principal financial and business centres. To the west, beyond [Deptford Creek](/wiki/Deptford_Creek "Deptford Creek"), is [Deptford](/wiki/Deptford "Deptford"), an area known for its maritime history, cultural diversity, and creative industries. To the east is [Charlton](/wiki/Charlton,_London "Charlton, London"), a predominantly residential district with historic landmarks and extensive green spaces. The [Greenwich Peninsula](/wiki/Greenwich_Peninsula "Greenwich Peninsula") lies to the north-east and is a major regeneration area, home to large-scale residential developments, entertainment venues, and the [O2](/wiki/Millennium_Dome "Millennium Dome"). To the south are [Blackheath](/wiki/Blackheath,_London "Blackheath, London"), an affluent village characterised by its large open heath, and [Lewisham](/wiki/Lewisham "Lewisham"), a significant town centre and transport hub within south-east London undergoing redevelopment. To the south-west is [St John's](/wiki/St_John%27s,_London "St John's, London"), a mainly residential neighbourhood with Victorian housing, while to the south-east lies [Kidbrooke](/wiki/Kidbrooke "Kidbrooke"), an area with new housing and improved infrastructure.

## Sites of interest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Sites of interest")]

### Riverfront

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Riverfront")]

RFA *Argus* being towed to Greenwich in June 2017

The *Cutty Sark* (a [clipper](/wiki/Clipper "Clipper") ship) has been preserved in a dry dock by the river. A major fire in May 2007 destroyed a part of the ship, although much had already been removed for restoration. Nearby for many years was also displayed *[Gipsy Moth IV](/wiki/Gipsy_Moth_IV "Gipsy Moth IV")*, the 54 feet (16.5 m) yacht sailed by Sir [Francis Chichester](/wiki/Francis_Chichester "Francis Chichester") in his single-handed, 226-day [circumnavigation](/wiki/Circumnavigation "Circumnavigation") of the globe during 1966–67. In 2004, *Gipsy Moth IV* was removed from Greenwich, and after restoration work completed a second circumnavigation in May 2007. On the riverside in front of the north-west corner of the hospital is an obelisk erected in memory of [Arctic](/wiki/Arctic "Arctic") explorer [Joseph René Bellot](/wiki/Joseph_Ren%C3%A9_Bellot "Joseph René Bellot").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/GreatRiverRace08.JPG/250px-GreatRiverRace08.JPG)](/wiki/File:GreatRiverRace08.JPG)

Boats at Greenwich at the end of the Great River Race

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/HMS_Defender_at_Greenwich.jpg/250px-HMS_Defender_at_Greenwich.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMS_Defender_at_Greenwich.jpg)

The Royal Navy [Type 45 destroyer](/wiki/Type_45_destroyer "Type 45 destroyer") [HMS *Defender*](/wiki/HMS_Defender_(D36) "HMS Defender (D36)") moored on the riverfront at Greenwich in 2015

Near the Cutty Sark site, a cylindrical building contains the entrance to the [Greenwich foot tunnel](/wiki/Greenwich_foot_tunnel "Greenwich foot tunnel"), opened on 4 August 1902. This connects Greenwich to the [Isle of Dogs](/wiki/Isle_of_Dogs "Isle of Dogs") on the northern side of the River Thames. The north exit of the tunnel is at [Island Gardens](/wiki/Island_Gardens "Island Gardens"),[[39]](#cite_note-39) from where the famous view of [Greenwich Hospital](/wiki/Greenwich_Hospital_(London) "Greenwich Hospital (London)") painted by [Canaletto](/wiki/Canaletto "Canaletto") can be seen.

Rowing has been part of life on the river at Greenwich for hundreds of years and the first Greenwich Regatta was held in 1785. The annual [Great River Race](/wiki/Great_River_Race "Great River Race") along the Thames [Tideway](/wiki/Tideway "Tideway") finishes at the Cutty Sark. The nearby Trafalgar Rowing Centre in Crane Street is home to [Curlew](/wiki/Curlew_Rowing_Club "Curlew Rowing Club") and [Globe](/wiki/Globe_Rowing_Club "Globe Rowing Club") rowing clubs.

The [Old Royal Naval College](/wiki/Royal_Naval_College,_Greenwich "Royal Naval College, Greenwich") is Sir Christopher Wren's domed masterpiece at the centre of the heritage site. The site is administered by the Greenwich Foundation and several of the buildings are let to the [University of Greenwich](/wiki/University_of_Greenwich "University of Greenwich") and one, the King Charles block, to [Trinity College of Music](/wiki/Trinity_College_of_Music "Trinity College of Music"). Within the complex is the former college dining room, the Painted Hall, this was painted by [James Thornhill](/wiki/James_Thornhill "James Thornhill"), and the [Chapel of St Peter and St Paul](/wiki/St_Paul%27s_Chapel,_Greenwich "St Paul's Chapel, Greenwich"), with an interior designed by [James 'Athenian' Stuart](/wiki/James_%27Athenian%27_Stuart "James 'Athenian' Stuart"). The Naval College had a training reactor, the [JASON reactor](/wiki/JASON_reactor "JASON reactor"), within the King William building that was operational between 1962 and 1996. The reactor was decommissioned and removed in 1999.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Riverside_entrance_to_Trinity_Hospital%2C_Greenwich_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1164174.jpg/250px-Riverside_entrance_to_Trinity_Hospital%2C_Greenwich_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1164174.jpg)](/wiki/File:Riverside_entrance_to_Trinity_Hospital,_Greenwich_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1164174.jpg)

Trinity Hospital, Greenwich

To the east of the Naval College is the [Trinity Hospital](/wiki/Trinity_Hospital,_Greenwich "Trinity Hospital, Greenwich") almshouse, founded in 1613, the oldest surviving building in the town centre.[[41]](#cite_note-41) This is next to the massive brick walls and the landing stage of [Greenwich Power Station](/wiki/Greenwich_Power_Station "Greenwich Power Station"). Built between 1902 and 1910 as a coal-fired station to supply power to [London's tram system](/wiki/London_County_Council_Tramways "London County Council Tramways"), and later the [London Underground](/wiki/London_Underground "London Underground"), it is now oil- and gas-powered and serves as a backup station for London Underground.[[42]](#cite_note-42) East Greenwich also has a small park, [East Greenwich Pleasaunce](/wiki/East_Greenwich_Pleasaunce "East Greenwich Pleasaunce"), which was formerly the burial ground of Greenwich Hospital.

[The O2](/wiki/The_O2_(London) "The O2 (London)") (formerly the [Millennium Dome](/wiki/Millennium_Dome "Millennium Dome")) was built on part of the site of [East Greenwich Gas Works](/wiki/East_Greenwich_Gas_Works "East Greenwich Gas Works"), a disused [British Gas](/wiki/British_Gas_plc "British Gas plc") site on the Greenwich Peninsula.[[43]](#cite_note-43) It is next to [North Greenwich Underground station](/wiki/North_Greenwich_tube_station "North Greenwich tube station"), about 3 miles (4.8 km) north east from the Greenwich town centre, north west of [Charlton](/wiki/Charlton,_Greenwich "Charlton, Greenwich"). Pear Tree Wharf was associated with the gas works, being used to unload coal for the manufacturing of town gas, and is now home to the [Greenwich Yacht Club](/wiki/Greenwich_Yacht_Club "Greenwich Yacht Club"). The [Greenwich Millennium Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Millennium_Village "Greenwich Millennium Village") is a modern [urban regeneration](/wiki/Urban_renewal "Urban renewal") development to the south of the Dome. [Enderby's Wharf](/wiki/Enderby%27s_Wharf "Enderby's Wharf") is a site associated with [submarine cable](/wiki/Submarine_communications_cable "Submarine communications cable") manufacture for over 150 years.

### Greenwich Park

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Greenwich Park")]

Main article: [Greenwich Park](/wiki/Greenwich_Park "Greenwich Park")

South of the former Naval College is the National Maritime Museum housed in buildings forming another symmetrical group and grand arcade incorporating the Queen's House, designed by Inigo Jones. Continuing to the south, Greenwich Park is a [Royal Park](/wiki/Royal_Parks_of_London "Royal Parks of London") of 183 acres (0.7 km2), laid out in the 17th century and formed from the hunting grounds of the Royal Palace of Placentia.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/TulipStair_QueensHouse_Greenwich.jpg/330px-TulipStair_QueensHouse_Greenwich.jpg)](/wiki/File:TulipStair_QueensHouse_Greenwich.jpg)

Spiral staircase and lantern at the Queen's House in Greenwich

The park rises towards Blackheath and at the top of this hill is a [statue](/wiki/Statue_of_James_Wolfe "Statue of James Wolfe") of [James Wolfe](/wiki/James_Wolfe "James Wolfe"), commander of the British expedition to capture [Quebec](/wiki/French_Quebec "French Quebec").[[45]](#cite_note-45) Nearby a major group of buildings within the park includes the former Royal Observatory, Greenwich; the Prime Meridian passes through this building.

[Greenwich Mean Time](/wiki/Greenwich_Mean_Time "Greenwich Mean Time") was at one time based on the time observations made at the Royal Greenwich Observatory, before being superseded by the closely related [Coordinated Universal Time](/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time "Coordinated Universal Time") (UTC). While there is no longer a working astronomical observatory at Greenwich, a [ball](/wiki/Time_ball "Time ball") still drops daily to mark the exact moment of 1 p.m., and there is a museum of astronomical and navigational tools, particularly [John Harrison](/wiki/John_Harrison "John Harrison")'s [marine chronometers](/wiki/Marine_chronometer "Marine chronometer").[[46]](#cite_note-46)

The [Ranger's House](/wiki/Ranger%27s_House "Ranger's House") lies at the Blackheath end of the park and houses the Wernher Collection of art,[[47]](#cite_note-47) and many fine houses, including [Vanbrugh's house](/wiki/John_Vanbrugh "John Vanbrugh") lie on Maze Hill, on the western edge of the park.

### Town centre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Town centre")]

[![A curving street with older two- and three-storey buildings on either side. In front is a black London taxicab with an advert; midway down the street is an intersection with heavy traffic. A cupolaed clock tower rises in the rear](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Downtown_Greenwich%2C_England_2.jpg/250px-Downtown_Greenwich%2C_England_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Downtown_Greenwich,_England_2.jpg)

Town centre

Around the covered Greenwich Market, Georgian and Victorian architecture dominates in the town centre which spreads to the west of the park and Royal Naval College. Up the hill from the centre, there are many streets of Georgian houses, including the [Fan Museum](/wiki/Fan_Museum "Fan Museum"), on Croom's Hill. Nearby, at the junction of Croom's Hill with Nevada Street, is Greenwich Theatre; at the eastern end of Nevada Street is the [Greenwich Tavern](/wiki/Greenwich_Tavern "Greenwich Tavern"). To the west, the arthouse Greenwich Cinema is on Greenwich High Road, while the nearby [Greenwich Playhouse](/wiki/Greenwich_Playhouse "Greenwich Playhouse") closed in 2012.

#### Market

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Market")]

[![An interior of a building with a translucent glass roof supported by blue-painted steel latticework. On the main floor are a number of different stalls with customers inspecting various wares.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Greenwich_Market_interior.jpg/250px-Greenwich_Market_interior.jpg)](/wiki/File:Greenwich_Market_interior.jpg)

Greenwich Market

There has been a market at Greenwich since the 14th century, but the history of the present market dates from 1700 when a charter to run two markets, on Wednesdays and Saturdays, was assigned by Lord Romney ([Henry, Earl of Romney](/wiki/Henry_Sydney,_1st_Earl_of_Romney "Henry Sydney, 1st Earl of Romney"))[[20]](#cite_note-Green-20) to the Commissioners of Greenwich Hospital for 1000 years.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

The market is part of "the island site", bounded by College Approach, Greenwich Church Street, [King William Walk](/wiki/King_William_Walk "King William Walk") and Nelson Road, near the National Maritime Museum and the Royal Observatory. The buildings surrounding the market are [Grade 2 listed](/wiki/Grade_2_listed "Grade 2 listed") and were established in 1827–1833 under the direction of [Joseph Kay](/wiki/Joseph_Kay_(architect) "Joseph Kay (architect)").[[49]](#cite_note-Heritage-49)[[50]](#cite_note-Phantom-50) A market roof was added in 1902–1908 (and replaced in 2016). Later significant development occurred in 1958–1960 and during the 1980s.

The landowner, Greenwich Hospital, enhanced the market between 2014 and early 2016. Following the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom") in 2020 the rents for several of the market stalls were increased by up to 60% as Greenwich Hospital's managing agent [Knight Frank](/wiki/Knight_Frank "Knight Frank") said it was losing money with fewer stalls operating and only four days of trading a week.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

### Millennium Leisure Park

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Millennium Leisure Park")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Millennium_Leisure_Park%2C_East_Greenwich_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1163888.jpg/250px-Millennium_Leisure_Park%2C_East_Greenwich_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1163888.jpg)](/wiki/File:Millennium_Leisure_Park,_East_Greenwich_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1163888.jpg)

Millennium Leisure Park

About 1.0 mile (1.6 km) east of Greenwich town centre, the Millennium Leisure Park is an out-of-town [retail park](/wiki/Retail_park "Retail park") on Bugsby's Way in east Greenwich. It consists of retail outlets ([IKEA](/wiki/IKEA "IKEA") and [B&Q](/wiki/B%26Q "B&Q")), restaurants and an [Odeon](/wiki/Odeon_Cinemas "Odeon Cinemas") cinema. The IKEA store here opened in 2019 as the retailer's fourth main store in London,[[52]](#cite_note-52) following stores in [Wembley](/wiki/Wembley "Wembley") (1988), [Croydon](/wiki/Croydon "Croydon") (1992) and [Tottenham](/wiki/Tottenham "Tottenham") (2005); the Greenwich store is the first in [Inner London](/wiki/Inner_London "Inner London").[[53]](#cite_note-53)

Greenwich Shopping Park is about 0.5 miles (0.8 km) further east, in Charlton.

## Greenwich Mean Time

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Greenwich Mean Time")]

Main article: [Greenwich Mean Time](/wiki/Greenwich_Mean_Time "Greenwich Mean Time")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Greenwich-Royal_Observatory-016.jpg/250px-Greenwich-Royal_Observatory-016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Greenwich-Royal_Observatory-016.jpg)

The [Royal Observatory, Greenwich](/wiki/Royal_Observatory,_Greenwich "Royal Observatory, Greenwich"), with the [time ball](/wiki/Time_ball "Time ball") atop the Octagon Room

[Greenwich Mean Time](/wiki/Greenwich_Mean_Time "Greenwich Mean Time") (GMT) is a term originally referring to [mean solar time](/wiki/Solar_time "Solar time") at the [Royal Observatory, Greenwich](/wiki/Royal_Observatory,_Greenwich "Royal Observatory, Greenwich"), which overlooks the River Thames from a hill in Greenwich Park. GMT is commonly used in practice to refer to [Coordinated Universal Time](/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time "Coordinated Universal Time") (UTC) when this is viewed as a [time zone](/wiki/Time_zone "Time zone"), especially by bodies connected with the United Kingdom, such as the [BBC World Service](/wiki/BBC_World_Service "BBC World Service"),[[54]](#cite_note-54) the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy"), the [Met Office](/wiki/Met_Office "Met Office") and others, although strictly UTC is an [atomic](/wiki/Atomic_clock "Atomic clock") [time scale](/wiki/Time_standard "Time standard") which only approximates GMT with a tolerance of 0.9 second. It is also used to refer to [Universal Time](/wiki/Universal_Time "Universal Time") (UT), which is a standard astronomical concept used in many technical fields and is often referred to by the military in the phrase [Zulu time](/wiki/Zulu_time "Zulu time").

As the United Kingdom grew into an advanced [maritime nation](/wiki/Maritime_nation "Maritime nation"), British mariners kept at least one chronometer on GMT in order to calculate their [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") from the [Greenwich meridian](/wiki/Prime_meridian_(Greenwich) "Prime meridian (Greenwich)"), which was by convention considered to have longitude zero degrees (this convention was internationally adopted in the [International Meridian Conference](/wiki/International_Meridian_Conference "International Meridian Conference") of 1884).[[note 1]](#cite_note-56) The synchronization of the chronometer on GMT did not affect shipboard time itself, which was still solar time. But this practice, combined with mariners from other nations drawing from [Nevil Maskelyne](/wiki/Nevil_Maskelyne "Nevil Maskelyne")'s method of [lunar distances](/wiki/Lunar_distance_(navigation) "Lunar distance (navigation)") based on observations at Greenwich, eventually led to GMT being used worldwide as a reference time independent of location. Most time zones were based upon this reference as a number of hours and half-hours "ahead of GMT" or "behind GMT".

In recognition of the suburb's astronomical links, [Asteroid 2830](/wiki/2830_Greenwich "2830 Greenwich") has been named 'Greenwich'.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

## World Heritage Site

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: World Heritage Site")]

In 1997 [Maritime Greenwich](/wiki/Old_Royal_Naval_College "Old Royal Naval College") was added to the list of [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO"), for the concentration and quality of buildings of historic and architectural interest. These can be divided into the group of buildings along the riverfront, Greenwich Park and the [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_era "Georgian era") and [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era") town centre. The World Heritage Site is described as:

> The ensemble of buildings at Greenwich, an outlying district of London, and the park in which they are set, symbolize English artistic and scientific endeavour in the 17th and 18th centuries. The Queen's House (by Inigo Jones) was the first Palladian building in England, while the complex that was until recently the Royal Naval College was designed by Christopher Wren. The park, laid out on the basis of an original design by André Le Nôtre, contains the Old Royal Observatory, the work of Wren and the scientist Robert Hooke.

— [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO")[[57]](#cite_note-UNESCO-58)

The area was selected by UNESCO on the criteria (i)(ii)(iv)(vi), recognizing it as a masterpiece of human creative genius, an important expression of intercultural exchange, an outstanding example of a significant stage in human history, and as being directly associated with events, traditions, or ideas of outstanding universal significance.[[57]](#cite_note-UNESCO-58)

  Reservoir designed by [Nicholas Hawksmoor](/wiki/Nicholas_Hawksmoor "Nicholas Hawksmoor"), fed by the historic underground waterways in [Greenwich Park](/wiki/Greenwich_Park "Greenwich Park"), it once supplied water to Greenwich Hospital.[[72]](#cite_note-73)
* [![Trafalgar Tavern](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Bunting_at_Trafalgar_Tavern%2C_Greenwich_-_2024-04-01_%281%29.jpg/250px-Bunting_at_Trafalgar_Tavern%2C_Greenwich_-_2024-04-01_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bunting_at_Trafalgar_Tavern,_Greenwich_-_2024-04-01_(1).jpg "The Trafalgar Tavern, built in 1837 by architect Joseph Kay, became a fashionable Victorian riverside destination attracting politicians and writers like Charles Dickens.[73]")

  The [Trafalgar Tavern](/wiki/Trafalgar_Tavern "Trafalgar Tavern"), built in 1837 by architect [Joseph Kay](/wiki/Joseph_Kay_(architect) "Joseph Kay (architect)"), became a fashionable [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era") riverside destination attracting politicians and writers like [Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens").[[73]](#cite_note-74)

Buildings located within the boundaries of the Zone of Legal Protection of **Maritime Greenwich**.[[74]](#cite_note-75)

Following the designation, property values in the surrounding area rose sharply, reflecting increased international visibility, tourism, and investor interest associated with World Heritage status. Greenwich in particular experienced accelerated gentrification, and the area west of [Greenwich Park](/wiki/Greenwich_Park "Greenwich Park") – notably within the West Greenwich Conservation Area – increasingly developed into some of the more affluent residential districts of [south-east London](/wiki/South-east_London "South-east London"), with rising house prices, redevelopment of historic buildings, and an influx of higher-income residents reshaping the local social and economic profile. In 2025, *The Telegraph* named West Greenwich as the 4th best place to live in London (after [Richmond Green](/wiki/Richmond_Green "Richmond Green"), [Marylebone](/wiki/Marylebone "Marylebone"), and [Hampstead](/wiki/Hampstead "Hampstead")).[[3]](#cite_note-Telegraph_Best_Places_London-3)

## Discover Greenwich Visitor Centre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Discover Greenwich Visitor Centre")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Samuel_pepys_building_greenwich.jpg/250px-Samuel_pepys_building_greenwich.jpg)](/wiki/File:Samuel_pepys_building_greenwich.jpg)

Pepys Building

The Discover Greenwich Visitor Centre provides an introduction to the history and attractions in the Greenwich World Heritage Site.[[75]](#cite_note-76) It is in The Pepys Building near to the *Cutty Sark* within the grounds of the [Old Royal Naval College](/wiki/Old_Royal_Naval_College "Old Royal Naval College") (formerly Greenwich Hospital); the building began life as an engineering laboratory for the college. The centre opened in March 2010, and admission is free.

The Centre explains the history of Greenwich as a royal residence and a maritime centre. Exhibits include:

* The history of the [Palace of Placentia](/wiki/Palace_of_Placentia "Palace of Placentia") on the site.
* Models of Christopher Wren's original designs for Greenwich Hospital.
* Six of the carved heads originally intended to decorate the exterior of the college's Painted Hall.
* Exhibition displays about Maritime Greenwich and its connections with the sea and exploration.
* *"By Wisdom as much as War"* – an exhibition about the history of the Royal Naval College during the years it occupied Greenwich Hospital (1873–1998).

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Education")]

Main article: [List of schools in Greenwich](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_Greenwich "List of schools in Greenwich")

The [University of Greenwich](/wiki/University_of_Greenwich "University of Greenwich") main campus occupies most of the grand, landmark riverside vista buildings of the former [Royal Naval College](/wiki/Royal_Naval_College,_Greenwich "Royal Naval College, Greenwich"). The university has other campuses at Avery Hill in [Eltham](/wiki/Eltham "Eltham") and at [Medway](/wiki/Medway "Medway"). The Greenwich campus also houses the [Trinity College of Music](/wiki/Trinity_College_of_Music "Trinity College of Music").

Secondary schools in the area include [The John Roan School](/wiki/The_John_Roan_School "The John Roan School"), founded 1677, and [St Ursula's Convent School](/wiki/St_Ursula%27s_Convent_School "St Ursula's Convent School"), established 1850.

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Transport")]

### National Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: National Rail")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Greenwich_railway_station_%28crop%29.JPG/250px-Greenwich_railway_station_%28crop%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Greenwich_railway_station_(crop).JPG)

[Greenwich station](/wiki/Greenwich_station "Greenwich station")

Greenwich is served by [Greenwich](/wiki/Greenwich_railway_station "Greenwich railway station") and [Maze Hill](/wiki/Maze_Hill_railway_station "Maze Hill railway station") stations with [Southeastern](/wiki/Southeastern_(train_operating_company) "Southeastern (train operating company)") services to [London Cannon Street](/wiki/Cannon_Street_railway_station "Cannon Street railway station"), [Dartford](/wiki/Dartford_railway_station "Dartford railway station"), [Barnehurst](/wiki/Barnehurst_railway_station "Barnehurst railway station") and [Crayford](/wiki/Crayford_railway_station "Crayford railway station") as well as [Thameslink](/wiki/Govia_Thameslink_Railway "Govia Thameslink Railway") services to [Luton](/wiki/Luton_railway_station "Luton railway station") via [London Blackfriars](/wiki/Blackfriars_railway_station "Blackfriars railway station") and to [Rainham](/wiki/Rainham_railway_station_(Kent) "Rainham railway station (Kent)").

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: London Underground")]

The area is also served by North Greenwich station with [Jubilee Line](/wiki/Jubilee_Line "Jubilee Line") services to [Stanmore](/wiki/Stanmore_tube_station "Stanmore tube station") and [Stratford](/wiki/Stratford_tube_station "Stratford tube station").

### Boat

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Boat")]

There are a number of river boat services running from [Greenwich Pier](/wiki/Greenwich_Pier "Greenwich Pier"), managed by [London River Services](/wiki/London_River_Services "London River Services"). The main services include the Thames commuter catamaran service run by [Thames Clippers](/wiki/Thames_Clippers "Thames Clippers") from [Embankment](/wiki/Embankment_Pier "Embankment Pier"), via [Tower Millennium Pier](/wiki/Tower_Millennium_Pier "Tower Millennium Pier"), [Canary Wharf](/wiki/Canary_Wharf_Pier "Canary Wharf Pier") and on to the [O2](/wiki/The_O2_(London) "The O2 (London)") and [Woolwich Arsenal Pier](/wiki/Woolwich_Arsenal_Pier "Woolwich Arsenal Pier");[[76]](#cite_note-77) the [Westminster](/wiki/Westminster_Millennium_Pier "Westminster Millennium Pier")-Greenwich cruise service by [Thames River Services](/wiki/Thames_River_Services "Thames River Services"); and the City Cruises tourist cruise via Westminster, [Waterloo](/wiki/Waterloo_Millennium_Pier "Waterloo Millennium Pier") and Tower piers.[[77]](#cite_note-78)

### Pedestrian and cycle routes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Pedestrian and cycle routes")]

The [Thames Path](/wiki/Thames_Path "Thames Path") [National Trail](/wiki/National_Trail "National Trail") runs along the riverside.[[78]](#cite_note-79) The Greenwich foot tunnel provides pedestrian access to the southern end of the Isle of Dogs, across the river Thames.

The [National Cycle Network](/wiki/National_Cycle_Network "National Cycle Network") Route 1 includes the foot tunnel, though cycling is not permitted in the tunnel itself.[[79]](#cite_note-80)

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Sports")]

### Rowing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Rowing")]

Greenwich is home to a variety of amateur sports clubs. Its location on the tidal Thames makes it a good location for rowing; the Trafalgar Rowing Centre in Crane Street is the clubhouse of the Curlew and Globe rowing clubs.[[80]](#cite_note-CurlewTRC-81)[[81]](#cite_note-82) The Globe has senior and junior squads, the latter renowned for its achievements at national and international level.[[82]](#cite_note-83)

### Running

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Running")]

The Thames Path and Greenwich Park are popular with runners. The 'red start' for the [London Marathon](/wiki/London_Marathon "London Marathon") is situated south of the park on Charlton Way (other starts are nearby in St John's Park, and on Shooter's Hill Road).[[83]](#cite_note-Route-84) After heading east through Charlton and Woolwich, the marathon route then turns west towards Greenwich; as runners reach the 10 km mark (6.2-mile), they pass the Old Royal Naval College and then loop around the prow of the *Cutty Sark* before continuing west towards Deptford.[[83]](#cite_note-Route-84)

### Golf

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Golf")]

The Greenwich Peninsula Golf Range at North Greenwich is a riverside golf driving range with 60 bays, a mini 18-hole adventure course, golf academy, golf shop and restaurant.[[84]](#cite_note-85)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Media")]

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Music")]

Greenwich's musical heritage also includes the historic role of music in its institutions. The Royal Borough is home to the [Trinity Laban Conservatoire of Music and Dance](/wiki/Trinity_Laban_Conservatoire_of_Music_and_Dance "Trinity Laban Conservatoire of Music and Dance"), a leading UK conservatoire formed by the 2005 merger of the Trinity College of Music and the Laban Dance Centre; its Greenwich site has educated internationally recognised musicians and composers. The [Queen's House](/wiki/Queen%27s_House "Queen's House") hosts classical concerts and early music performances, including [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") and [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") programmes in its Great Hall.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Greenwich_Theatre%2C_London_7_June_2011.jpg/250px-Greenwich_Theatre%2C_London_7_June_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Greenwich_Theatre,_London_7_June_2011.jpg)

The [Greenwich Theatre](/wiki/Greenwich_Theatre "Greenwich Theatre").

Greenwich has a long tradition of performance venues influencing its musical life. [St Alfege Church](/wiki/St_Alfege_Church "St Alfege Church") has a long tradition of sacred and [choral music](/wiki/Choral_music "Choral music"), hosting regular services with sung liturgy, a well-regarded choir led by a Director of Music, and free lunchtime recitals that feature classical and vocal performances; the church is also notable as the burial place of the 16th-century composer [Thomas Tallis](/wiki/Thomas_Tallis "Thomas Tallis").[[85]](#cite_note-86) The [Greenwich Theatre](/wiki/Greenwich_Theatre "Greenwich Theatre") traces its origins to the 19th century as the Rose and Crown Music Hall and later Crowder's Music Hall, showcasing concerts, burlesque and variety acts.

The wider cultural scene also intersects with music through multidisciplinary festivals like the [Greenwich+Docklands International Festival](/wiki/Greenwich%2BDocklands_International_Festival "Greenwich+Docklands International Festival"), which blends outdoor performance art with music during its annual summer programme. Greenwich has also been referenced directly in contemporary [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") work: South London keyboardist, composer and producer [James Beckwith](/wiki/James_Beckwith_(musician) "James Beckwith (musician)") titled his album *SE10* after the Greenwich postcode, describing it as dedicated to the Greenwich area where he lives and studied at Trinity Laban.[[86]](#cite_note-87)

Inheriting from the [dynamic electronic music scene in south-east London](/wiki/UK_garage "UK garage"), the area has also been used as a setting for live music and promotional events in the genre. The [Royal Naval College](/wiki/Old_Royal_Naval_College "Old Royal Naval College") is the location of the [electronic music](/wiki/Electronic_music "Electronic music") [festival](/wiki/Festival "Festival") *Labyrinth on the Thames*, using the river and its surroundings in Greenwich as a backdrop for an immersive musical experience.[[87]](#cite_note-Labyrinth_on_Thames-88) Its past headliners include the Grammy Award–winning DJ *[Black Coffee](/wiki/Black_Coffee_(DJ) "Black Coffee (DJ)")*, the Australian [electronic](/wiki/Electronic_music "Electronic music") duo *[Empire of the Sun](/wiki/Empire_of_the_Sun_(band) "Empire of the Sun (band)")*, [deep house](/wiki/Deep_house "Deep house") and [techno](/wiki/Techno "Techno") DJ *[Solomun](/wiki/Solomun "Solomun")*, and [dance music](/wiki/Dance_music "Dance music") artist *[FISHER](/wiki/Fisher_(musician) "Fisher (musician)")*.[[87]](#cite_note-Labyrinth_on_Thames-88) More recent and announced performers include electronic acts *[Moby](/wiki/Moby "Moby")*, *[Dom Dolla](/wiki/Dom_Dolla "Dom Dolla")* and *[Overmono](/wiki/Overmono "Overmono")*, reflecting the festival's mix of internationally renowned DJs and live electronic acts.

### Film and television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Film and television")]

Greenwich, particularly the [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") and the [Royal Naval College](/wiki/Royal_Naval_College,_Greenwich "Royal Naval College, Greenwich"), have been used extensively as filming locations for film and television productions.[[88]](#cite_note-89)[[89]](#cite_note-90)

Notable productions filmed at the Royal Naval College include *[Thor: The Dark World](/wiki/Thor:_The_Dark_World "Thor: The Dark World")*, *[Les Misérables](/wiki/Les_Mis%C3%A9rables_(2012_film) "Les Misérables (2012 film)")*, *[Skyfall](/wiki/Skyfall "Skyfall")*, *[The Dark Knight Rises](/wiki/The_Dark_Knight_Rises "The Dark Knight Rises")*, and the television series *[Bridgerton](/wiki/Bridgerton "Bridgerton")* and *[The Crown](/wiki/The_Crown_(TV_series) "The Crown (TV series)")*.[[90]](#cite_note-91)[[91]](#cite_note-92) *[Bridgerton](/wiki/Bridgerton "Bridgerton")* was also filmed at the [Ranger's House](/wiki/Ranger%27s_House "Ranger's House").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Greenwich_Picturehouse_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1839263.jpg/250px-Greenwich_Picturehouse_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1839263.jpg)](/wiki/File:Greenwich_Picturehouse_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1839263.jpg)

The Greenwich Picturehouse.

In January 2026, entertainment press reported that filming for *Avengers: Doomsday* took place in Greenwich, including at the Royal Naval College.[[92]](#cite_note-93)

[Greenwich Picturehouse](/wiki/Picturehouse_Cinemas "Picturehouse Cinemas") is an independent [cinema](/wiki/Movie_theater "Movie theater") on Greenwich High Road that has played a notable role in the area's film culture. It originally opened in 1989 as the Greenwich Cinema, a purpose-built multi-screen venue, but closed in 2002 following increased competition from larger multiplex cinemas.[[93]](#cite_note-94) The building was subsequently refurbished and reopened in 2005 as Greenwich Picturehouse, expanding to five screens and adding a café and bar.[[94]](#cite_note-95) Since its reopening, the cinema has become an established cultural venue in the borough, screening a mix of mainstream releases, independent and foreign-language [films](/wiki/Films "Films"), [documentaries](/wiki/Documentaries "Documentaries"), and special events, and hosting festivals and community-focused programmes that contribute to Greenwich's wider cultural life.[[95]](#cite_note-96)

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greenwich&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Literature")]

* *Greenwich Park* (2021), a psychological thriller by Katherine Faulkner, is set in and around [Greenwich Park](/wiki/Greenwich_Park "Greenwich Park").[[96]](#cite_note-97)

* *On the Greenwich Line* (2019; English edition 2025), a novel by Shady Lewis, uses the Greenwich line/meridian as a key motif while exploring contemporary London life and bureaucracy.[[97]](#cite_note-98)

* *Greenwich: The Place Where Days Begin and End* (2000), a non-fiction history by Charles Jennings, discusses Greenwich’s development and global significance as the reference point for time and longitude.[[98]](#cite_note-99)

* [Edward Lear](/wiki/Edward_Lear "Edward Lear") makes reference to Greenwich in *[More Nonsense Pictures, Rhymes, Botany, etc](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/More_Nonsense_Pictures,_Rhymes,_Botany,_etc "s:More Nonsense Pictures, Rhymes, Botany, etc")*.[[99]](#cite_note-100)

He is best remembered for his command at the [Battle of the Plains of Abraham](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Plains_of_Abraham "Battle of the Plains of Abraham") in 1759 during the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"), which secured British control of [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada").

  [James Glaisher](/wiki/James_Glaisher "James Glaisher") (1809-1903) was based for much of his career at the [Royal Observatory](/wiki/Royal_Observatory,_Greenwich "Royal Observatory, Greenwich"), where he worked as a [meteorologist](/wiki/Meteorologist "Meteorologist"). He is best known for his pioneering [high-altitude balloon](/wiki/High-altitude_balloon "High-altitude balloon") ascents in the 1860s, which advanced the scientific study of the [atmosphere](/wiki/Atmosphere "Atmosphere"). He lived at 20 Dartmouth Hill.

As a co-founder of the [Aeronautical Society of Great Britain](/wiki/Aeronautical_Society_of_Great_Britain "Aeronautical Society of Great Britain"), he promoted early research into [heavier-than-air flight](/wiki/Heavier-than-air_flight "Heavier-than-air flight") and helped establish aviation as a scientific discipline.
